[![6 Battle of Mount Tabor (1799) on 16 April 1799](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Transparent_square.svg/20px-Transparent_square.svg.png)](/wiki/Battle_of_Mount_Tabor_(1799) "6 Battle of Mount Tabor (1799) on 16 April 1799")

[![5 Siege of Acre (1799) from 20 March to 21 May 1799](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Transparent_square.svg/20px-Transparent_square.svg.png)](/wiki/Siege_of_Acre_(1799) "5 Siege of Acre (1799) from 20 March to 21 May 1799")

(17 August – 2 September 1801) was fought during the [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars") between French and British forces. It was the last action of the [French invasion of Egypt and Syria](/wiki/French_invasion_of_Egypt_and_Syria "French invasion of Egypt and Syria") (1798–1801). The French had occupied [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria"), a major fortified harbour city on the [Nile](/wiki/Nile "Nile") Delta in northern [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), since 2 July 1798, and the garrison there surrendered on 2 September 1801.

current battle

Napoleon in command till 23 August 1799

The **siege of Alexandria** (17 August – 2 September 1801) was fought during the [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars") between French and British forces. It was the last action of the [French invasion of Egypt and Syria](/wiki/French_invasion_of_Egypt_and_Syria "French invasion of Egypt and Syria") (1798–1801). The French had occupied [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria"), a major fortified harbour city on the [Nile](/wiki/Nile "Nile") Delta in northern [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), since 2 July 1798, and the garrison there surrendered on 2 September 1801.

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Siege_of_Alexandria_(1801)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

Further information: [Siege of Cairo](/wiki/Siege_of_Cairo "Siege of Cairo")

The battle between the British and French at [Canope](/wiki/Battle_of_Alexandria_(1801) "Battle of Alexandria (1801)") on 21 March 1801 resulted in a French repulse. The French under Menou, disheartened by this failure, retired to Alexandria. With Abercromby's death, [John Hely-Hutchinson](/wiki/John_Hely-Hutchinson,_2nd_Earl_of_Donoughmore "John Hely-Hutchinson, 2nd Earl of Donoughmore") succeeded as commander of the British force in August. He now intended to lay siege to Alexandria and bottle Menou up.

Hutchinson left [Coote](/wiki/Eyre_Coote_(British_Army_officer) "Eyre Coote (British Army officer)") with 6,000 men and then sent part of the reserve with [Baron Charles de Hompesch](/w/index.php?title=Charles_De_Hompesh&action=edit&redlink=1 "Charles De Hompesh (page does not exist)") to capture [Rosetta](/wiki/Rosetta "Rosetta"). He then advanced to [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo"), which he reached, after a few skirmishes, in mid June. Joined by a sizable Ottoman force, Hutchinson invested Cairo and on 27 June the 13,000-strong French garrison under General [Augustin Daniel Belliard](/wiki/Augustin_Daniel_Belliard "Augustin Daniel Belliard"), out-manned and out-gunned, surrendered. General [John Moore](/wiki/John_Moore_(British_Army_officer) "John Moore (British Army officer)") then escorted them to the coast via Rosetta.[[2]](#cite_note-Barthorp_p._6-2)

## Siege

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Siege_of_Alexandria_(1801)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Siege")]

Hutchinson, with Cairo out of the way, now began the final reduction of Alexandria. He had thirty five battalions in total. While the reserve feinted to the east, Coote, with the Guards and two other brigades, landed on 16 August to its west where fierce opposition was encountered by the garrison of [Fort Marabout](/w/index.php?title=Fort_Marabout&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fort Marabout (page does not exist)"), which the [54th Regiment of Foot](/wiki/54th_(West_Norfolk)_Regiment_of_Foot "54th (West Norfolk) Regiment of Foot") eventually stormed. Both sides mounted combined assaults but the French soldiers, unable to break out and with food shortages and disease taking their toll, became increasingly disillusioned with the campaign. Menou knew he had no hope and on 26 August asked for terms; on 26 August he proposed formal terms of [capitulation](/wiki/Capitulation_(surrender) "Capitulation (surrender)"). The terms as amended by British commanders and put into effect are known as the [Capitulation of Alexandria](/wiki/Capitulation_of_Alexandria_(1801) "Capitulation of Alexandria (1801)").

## Aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Siege_of_Alexandria_(1801)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Aftermath")]

By 2 September, 10,000 French troops surrendered under terms which allowed them to keep their personal weapons and baggage, and to return to France on British ships. However, all French ships and cannons at Alexandria were surrendered to the victors. French warships captured in the harbour were divided between the British and Ottomans. The [frigates](/wiki/Frigate "Frigate") [*Égyptienne*](/wiki/French_frigate_%C3%89gyptienne_(1799) "French frigate Égyptienne (1799)"), [*Régénérée*](/wiki/French_frigate_R%C3%A9g%C3%A9n%C3%A9r%C3%A9e_(1794) "French frigate Régénérée (1794)") and *Léoben* went to Britain, while the frigates [*Justice*](/wiki/French_frigate_Justice_(1795) "French frigate Justice (1795)") and *Mantoue*, [hospital ship](/wiki/Hospital_ship "Hospital ship") *[Causse](/wiki/San_Carlo_Borromeo-class_ship_of_the_line#Ships "San Carlo Borromeo-class ship of the line")* and corvettes *Halil Bey*, *Momgo Balerie* and *Salâbetnümâ* went to the Ottomans.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Historians relate that the French garrison, feeling abandoned by an uncaring Republic, gradually abandoned the high standards of conduct and service characteristic of the [French Revolutionary Army](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Army "French Revolutionary Army"). Many soldiers refused to renew their oath to the Republic, or did so half-heartedly.[[4]](#cite_note-4) In his memoirs, the surgeon-in-chief of Napoleon's Grand Army, Baron [Dominique-Jean Larrey](/wiki/Dominique-Jean_Larrey "Dominique-Jean Larrey"), remembers how the consumption of the [meat of young Arab horses](/wiki/Horse_meat "Horse meat") helped the French to curb an epidemic of [scurvy](/wiki/Scurvy "Scurvy"). He would so start the 19th-century tradition of horse meat consumption in France.[[5]](#cite_note-Musee-5)

## The Rosetta Stone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Siege_of_Alexandria_(1801)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: The Rosetta Stone")]

After the surrender, a dispute arose over the fate of French archaeological and scientific discoveries in Egypt. One of the key artifacts was the [Rosetta Stone](/wiki/Rosetta_Stone "Rosetta Stone") which had been discovered in mid-July 1799 by French scientists of the [Institut d'Égypte](/wiki/Institut_d%27%C3%89gypte "Institut d'Égypte"). Menou refused to hand them over, claiming they belonged to the institute. How exactly the stone came into British hands is disputed. Colonel [Tomkyns Hilgrove Turner](/wiki/Tomkyns_Hilgrove_Turner "Tomkyns Hilgrove Turner"), who escorted the stone to Britain, claimed later that he had personally seized it from Menou and carried it away on a [gun carriage](/wiki/Caisson_(military) "Caisson (military)").[[6]](#cite_note-6) Turner brought the stone to Britain aboard *Egyptienne*, landing in February 1802. On 11 March it was presented to the [Society of Antiquaries of London](/wiki/Society_of_Antiquaries_of_London "Society of Antiquaries of London"). Later it was taken to the [British Museum](/wiki/British_Museum "British Museum"), where it remains to this day. Inscriptions painted in white on the artifact state "Captured in Egypt by the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") in 1801" on the left side and "Presented by King [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III")" on the right.
